rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE HOM FAMILY TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Quash Or Alternatively Motion For Protective Order And Supporting Memorandum
rulings.Rulings: Brian and Anna Hom's Motion to Quash is denied in part and granted in part. The Motion to Quash is not procedurally defective for lack of a separate statement given CRC 3.1345(b). If the subpoenaed banks possess responsive records which indicate on the face of such records that deposits were in the form of cash, the subpoenaed records reflecting cash deposits of $300 or more, made between June 2010 and April 2014, into the specified accounts shall be produced. To the extent bank records do not state whether deposits were in the form of cash, they shall not be produced. The banks may redact all but the last four digits of any account numbers and the names of any non-party joint account holders; and, in any event, such information shall be redacted from the records and not be communicated to anyone else by the attorney receiving any such records. Any responsive records produced are subject to the Protective Order the Court adopted on March 15, 2018, and any dissemination beyond attorneys is subject to the procedures provided in that order. All responses and production of information and records must be made within 20 days of this order. No sanctions are awarded. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, January 9, 2019.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE ANN L. HILDULA TRUST UDT DATED 2/28/2013 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement
rulings.Rulings: Morgan Hildula's Petition to Void the Settlement Agreement (which is treated as a motion to vacate the order approving the settlement) is denied, and Paul Thiebeault's Motion to Enforce Settlement Agreement is granted. Morgan's opposition to the latter motion is premised on the invalidity of the order approving the settlement. The time to appeal or seek reconsideration of this Court's order approving the settlement has expired, and Morgan has failed to show either extrinsic fraud or extrinsic mistake in connection with that order. The Court's determination that the settlement was in Morgan's best interests was implicit in the Court's approval order, was not required to be made explicit, and was not negated by the Court's striking of the overbroad sentence relating to the best interests of all parties. The issue of whether the settlement was in Morgan's best interests was fully briefed and was argued at the hearing on the petition to approve. Arguments for and against were before the Court and were considered. Petitioner was represented by her father, Scott Hildula, as guardian ad litem, and who, in turn, was represented by counsel, Edward Koplowitz. Given Mr. Koplowitz's appearance and argument at the hearing, there was no need for either Scott or Morgan Hildula to attend the hearing (at which no evidence was taken), and there was good cause as contemplated by California Rule of Court 7.952 for the Court to excuse their personal presence. Even assuming there was a requirement that the petition to approve the settlement be submitted on a Judicial Council form, there was no prejudice from such a form not being used. Even assuming there was a requirement that the order approving the settlement as to Morgan be on a Judicial Council form, there was no prejudice to Morgan from such a form not being used (and the arguably applicable Judicial Council form order does not explicitly state that the settlement is in the minor's best interests). "An otherwise legally sufficient court order for which there is a mandatory Judicial Council form is not invalid or unenforceable because the order is not prepared on a Judicial Council form or the correct Judicial Council form." California Rule of Court 1.31(g). Other issues argued by Mary Connolly in her Joinder are not properly before the Court on Morgan's petition. Mary is not a party to the settlement (which does not compromise any interest she has in her portion of the real property), and she has no claim on the property interests dealt with in the settlement. Nor is she a trustee of Ann's trust, notwithstanding a pending nomination (which is claimed to be a breach of the settlement). Even assuming she has a sufficient interest to allow joinder, she cannot through her joinder expand the issues presented by Morgan's petition. In any event, the issues Mary argues do not rise to the level of extrinsic fraud or mistake. The disagreement as to whether Ann Hildula effectively transferred property out of her trust were known to the parties and had been presented prior to the settlement and its approval. Indeed, the settlement-which on its face allocated the full 50% real property interest to which Paul Thibeault, Joseph Hildula, and Morgan Hildula could potentially lay claim-was intended to address the risks inherent in that disagreement and the expense and delay that would be involved in litigating the issue to a final conclusion as among the settling parties. The Court will not consider arguments first made in reply briefs, which, in any event, would not change the outcome of these motions. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, January 9, 2019.
